mcqs.explanation: The add(int index, E element) method inserts element at the specified index, shifting existing elements to the right. So [1,2] becomes [1,5,2] after inserting 5 at index 1.

mcqs.question: Which collection class would provide the BEST performance for frequent insertions and deletions at both ends?
mcqs.explanation: LinkedList provides O(1) insertion/deletion at both ends because it's a doubly-linked list. ArrayDeque is O(1) at ends but doesn't allow random access or modification in the middle. ArrayList is O(n) for insertions at the front.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the contains() method in a HashSet?
mcqs.explanation: HashSet uses hash-based storage. Average case is O(1) but worst case (when all elements hash to same bucket - hash collision) becomes O(n). With good hash function and load factor, worst case is rare.

mcqs.question: Consider a TreeSet containing {5, 2, 8, 1, 9}. If we iterate using an iterator, what is the order of elements retrieved?
mcqs.explanation: TreeSet uses a Red-Black tree implementation and maintains elements in sorted (natural) order. When iterating, elements are retrieved in ascending sorted order: 1, 2, 5, 8, 9.

mcqs.question: What will happen when trying to add a duplicate element to a HashSet?

options.text: The set remains unchanged, add() returns false

mcqs.explanation: HashSet does not allow duplicates. When add() is called with a duplicate, it returns false and the set remains unchanged. No exception is thrown - this is by design.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid way to iterate over a List in Java?

options.text: for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) System.out.println(list.get(i));

mcqs.explanation: Option D is misleading - forEach() doesn't provide index access by default. All other methods are valid. To iterate with index, you'd use IntStream.range(0, list.size()).forEach(i -> ...).
HashMap does not allow duplicate keys. When put() is called with an existing key ("A"), it replaces the value. Size remains 2 (keys A and B), and get("A") returns 3 (the new value).

mcqs.question: Which interface does java.util.Collections class implement?

options.text: None - it's a utility class with static methods

mcqs.explanation: Collections is a utility class (all static methods) that provides algorithms for collection manipulation like sorting, searching, reversing, shuffling, etc. It doesn't implement any collection interface.

clone() creates a shallow copy - the ArrayList object is new but elements inside are references to the same objects. Since we're only adding to al2 (not modifying shared objects), al remains size 2, al2 becomes size 3.

mcqs.question: Which of these collections allows null elements?
mcqs.explanation: ArrayList allows null elements (and multiple nulls since duplicates allowed). TreeSet and TreeMap don't allow null (TreeMap throws NullPointerException, TreeSet throws NullPointerException or ClassCastException). Hashtable doesn't allow null keys or values (synchronized legacy class).

mcqs.question: What does Collections.sort() use internally for sorting?
mcqs.explanation: Since Java 7, Collections.sort() uses TimSort (a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort), which is stable and performs O(n log n). Previously Java used a modified mergesort (also stable).

mcqs.question: Given: List<String> list = Arrays.asList("A", "B", "C"); What happens when we execute list.add("D")?

options.text: Compiles but fails at runtime with IndexOutOfBoundsException

mcqs.explanation: Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size list backed by the original array. It supports get() and set() but NOT add() or remove() - these throw UnsupportedOperationException.

mcqs.question: Which iterator method must be called before accessing the first element?

options.text: No method needed - next() gives first element

mcqs.explanation: Iterator does not have a method to 'reset' or get first element explicitly. The first call to next() returns the first element. hasNext() should be called first to check if elements exist.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the load factor in HashMap?

options.text: To determine when to resize the internal array
To limit maximum elements in the map

mcqs.explanation: Load factor determines the threshold for resizing: when (size > capacity × load factor), the HashMap is resized (doubles capacity and rehashes all entries). Default 0.75 balances time vs space usage.

mcqs.question: If a class implements Comparable, how many compareTo() implementations can it have?

options.text: Depends on number of fields in the class

mcqs.explanation: A class can implement Comparable only once, defining its natural ordering. For multiple sorting strategies, use Comparator (external comparison logic) which can be created as many times as needed.
